ruleName: Multiple authentication failures - single source IP
ruleDescription: Detects when a single source IP generates a high volume of failed authentications across multiple users within a short window. May indicate password spraying, credential stuffing, or a misconfigured client.
starterQuery:
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(6h)
| where IPAddress == "45.83.193.150"
| take 50
investigationGoals: Is this a single account or many?
groundTruthSummary: Password spray from 45.83.193.150 targeting 247 users with 1,840 attempts. 3 successful sign-ins.
correctActions: Force password reset on the 3 compromised accounts
redHerrings: Email all users to change passwords
playbookReason: Classic low-and-slow password spray. The smoking gun is dcount(UserPrincipalName) grouped by IP - one IP touching hundreds of users is never legitimate. Three successful logins means active foothold - escalate immediately.
hunterHints: Start by counting failures per IP and per user. The ratio tells you the technique.
Try: SigninLogs | where IPAddress == "45.83.193.150" | summarize Attempts=count(), Users=dcount(UserPrincipalName), Successes=countif(ResultType == "0") by IPAddress
Once you know it's spraying, find the successful ones: filter ResultType == "0".

ruleDescription: Sign-in detected from an IP categorised as Tor/proxy/anonymizer. Default risk indicator - context determines verdict.
starterQuery:
SigninLogs
| where IPAddress == "199.249.230.100"
| take 20
investigationGoals: What kind of account is it?
Has this account used anonymizers before?
Is the activity at expected time/scope?
groundTruthSummary: service-scanner is a documented OSINT service account that performs external scans via Tor. Matches scheduled window.
correctActions: Verify the service account purpose in CMDB
Confirm activity matches scheduled scan window
playbookReason: Always check what an account IS before reacting. Service accounts have documented exceptions. Killing this account would break the scanner.
hunterHints: The account name 'service-scanner' is a clue. Service accounts usually have a single purpose - check what it does.
AppDisplayName field tells you which application the sign-in was for.

ruleDescription: Two sign-ins for the same user from geographically distant locations within a window that is physically impossible to travel.
starterQuery:
SigninLogs
| where UserPrincipalName == "j.harrington@contoso.com"
| where TimeGenerated > ago(4h)
| project TimeGenerated, IPAddress, City, Location, ResultType, AppDisplayName
| sort by TimeGenerated asc
investigationGoals: What are the two locations and times?
Did MFA succeed in both cases?
Is the suspicious IP on a TI list?
groundTruthSummary: Hull, UK at 07:04 then Bucharest, RO at 07:42. 38 minutes apart - physically impossible. Suspicious IP is a known C2.
playbookReason: 38 minutes between Hull and Bucharest is impossible. Cross-reference the second IP against the TI table - it's a known C2. Containment first.
hunterHints: Pull every sign-in for this user in the last few hours and look at the City/Location columns.
When you find the suspicious IP, check it against the ThreatIntelligenceIndicator table.

ruleDescription: Detects when a user receives an abnormal number of MFA prompts within a short window. May indicate push-bombing / MFA fatigue attacks.
starterQuery:
SigninLogs
| where UserPrincipalName == "ceo@contoso.com"
| where TimeGenerated between (datetime(2026-05-13T02:00:00Z) .. datetime(2026-05-13T03:00:00Z))
| project TimeGenerated, IPAddress, City, ResultType, ResultDescription
| sort by TimeGenerated asc
investigationGoals: How many prompts in what timeframe?
What was the final outcome - did any succeed?
Where did the prompts come from?
groundTruthSummary: 38 MFA prompts to CEO between 02:30-02:45 from Bucharest IP. Prompt 36 was approved (likely tap-by-mistake at 02:45 AM). Account compromised.
correctActions: Switch user to number-matching MFA or FIDO2
playbookReason: MFA fatigue works by exhausting the user. Approval on attempt #36 at 02:45 AM is the breach. The durable fix is number-matching or hardware keys.
hunterHints: Use bin(TimeGenerated, 1m) and count() to see the prompt rate per minute.
ResultDescription tells you which prompt was approved vs denied.

ruleDescription: Sign-in detected using legacy authentication (POP, IMAP, SMTP). Legacy auth bypasses modern conditional access and MFA.
starterQuery:
SigninLogs
| where ClientAppUsed in ("POP", "IMAP", "SMTP", "Other clients")
| where TimeGenerated > ago(6h)
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, IPAddress, ClientAppUsed
groundTruthSummary: Internal printer service account using POP3 from printer subnet. Documented legacy exception.
playbookReason: Legacy auth is risky but printers exist. Documented exceptions get respected. Push migration without breaking the business.
hunterHints: The IP address tells you internal vs external. Internal RFC1918 ranges (10., 172.16-31., 192.168.) are inside.

ruleDescription: An Office application (Word, Excel, PowerPoint) created a child process that is a scripting engine (PowerShell, cmd, wscript). Common in phishing-driven macro attacks.
starterQuery:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where DeviceName == "WS-FIN-014"
| where InitiatingProcessFileName in~ ("WINWORD.EXE", "EXCEL.EXE", "POWERPNT.EXE")
| project Timestamp, FileName, ProcessCommandLine, InitiatingProcessCommandLine
investigationGoals: What did the child process do?
Where did the document come from (Outlook attachment?)
groundTruthSummary: WINWORD opened Invoice_April2026.docm from Outlook cache, spawned encoded PowerShell that contacts 185.220.101.42. Patient zero.
playbookReason: Office spawning PowerShell is the classic phishing chain. Trace forward: what did PowerShell do next? You'll find certutil, rundll32, the lot.
hunterHints: Pivot on the host: every malicious thing that happens next is on WS-FIN-014.
Sort by Timestamp ascending to build the timeline.
Look at InitiatingProcessFileName to follow the parent-child chain.

ruleDescription: Detects use of legitimate Windows binaries (certutil, bitsadmin, mshta, regsvr32) with command lines typical of malicious download or execution proxying.
starterQuery:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where DeviceName == "WS-FIN-014"
| where FileName in~ ("certutil.exe", "bitsadmin.exe", "mshta.exe", "regsvr32.exe")
| project Timestamp, FileName, ProcessCommandLine, AccountName
investigationGoals: What URL or file is it touching?
groundTruthSummary: certutil.exe -urlcache -split -f downloading payload.bin from 185.220.101.42. Parent process is the encoded PowerShell from INC-2026-0506.
playbookReason: certutil pulling from a raw IP is textbook LOLBin abuse. Connect to the parent process to confirm the attack chain.
hunterHints: The -urlcache flag is the giveaway - it downloads remote content.
The destination IP appears in other alerts. Pivot.

ruleName: LSASS process accessed by unusual binary
ruleDescription: A process other than known security tools accessed the memory of LSASS. Classic credential dumping behaviour.
starterQuery:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where DeviceName == "WS-FIN-014"
| where ProcessCommandLine has_any ("lsass", "comsvcs", "MiniDump")
| project Timestamp, FileName, ProcessCommandLine, AccountName
investigationGoals: What technique was used (mimikatz, comsvcs, procdump)?
Was the host already showing other badness?
groundTruthSummary: rundll32 + comsvcs.dll MiniDump against LSASS PID 712, output to C:\Users\Public\lsass.dmp. Active credential theft.
playbookReason: Every minute the host is online, more credentials are at risk. Isolation first, evidence second.
hunterHints: The comsvcs.dll MiniDump technique is a living-off-the-land credential dump - no extra tools required.
PID 712 is the LSASS process target.

ruleDescription: Sysmon Event ID 8: a process created a remote thread in another process. Often used to inject malicious code into a trusted process to evade detection.
starterQuery:
SysmonEvent
| where Computer == "WS-FIN-014"
| where EventID == 8
| project TimeGenerated, SourceImage, TargetImage
groundTruthSummary: powershell.exe injected a remote thread into explorer.exe. Classic stealth technique.
playbookReason: PS injecting into explorer is almost certainly malicious. Memory capture is essential - payload lives in RAM.
hunterHints: Sysmon EID 8 fields: SourceImage (the injector), TargetImage (the victim).
Look for other EID types on the same host - injection is rarely alone.

ruleDescription: Sysmon EID 17: named pipe created with a pattern matching known offensive tool defaults (e.g. Cobalt Strike's msagent_*).
starterQuery:
SysmonEvent
| where Computer == "WS-FIN-014"
| where EventID == 17
| project TimeGenerated, PipeName, Image
groundTruthSummary: msagent_57 pipe created by rundll32 - Cobalt Strike default pattern.
playbookReason: msagent_* is a Cobalt Strike default. Real adversaries forget to rename. High-confidence beacon indicator.
hunterHints: PipeName starting with 'msagent_' or 'postex_' is a Cobalt Strike calling card.

ruleDescription: Detects creation of Run keys, scheduled tasks, services, or WMI subscriptions - common persistence techniques.
starterQuery:
SysmonEvent
| where Computer == "WS-FIN-014"
| where EventID in (12, 13, 20, 21)
| project TimeGenerated, EventID, TargetObject, Details, Consumer, Destination
investigationGoals: What mechanism (Run key, task, WMI, service)?
groundTruthSummary: Three persistence mechanisms on same host: scheduled task 'GoogleUpdaterTask', Run key 'Updater', WMI consumer. All point to C:\Users\Public\svchost.exe.
playbookReason: Belt-and-braces persistence means re-image. Removing one mechanism just leaves the others.
hunterHints: EID 13 = registry value set. EID 20 = WMI consumer. DeviceProcessEvents shows schtasks.exe.
All three point to the same binary path - that's the smoking gun.

ruleDescription: Detects service stop events for known security tools (Sysmon, Defender, EDR agents).
starterQuery:
SysmonEvent
| where Computer == "WS-FIN-014"
| where EventID == 7036
| project TimeGenerated, ParameterXml
investigationGoals: What happened next on the host?
groundTruthSummary: Sysmon64 service stopped by SYSTEM on WS-FIN-014. No change request. Anti-forensics.
correctActions: Isolate via EDR (still online to MDE)
Force restart + re-enable Sysmon via GPO
playbookReason: Attacker is blinding defenders. Typically precedes deeper compromise like ransomware.
hunterHints: After a tool-stop event, look for what happens in the 10 minutes that follow. The attacker chose THIS moment for a reason.

ruleDescription: vssadmin or wmic used to delete shadow copies. Highest-fidelity pre-ransomware indicator.
starterQuery:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where DeviceName == "WS-FIN-014"
| where ProcessCommandLine has "vssadmin" and ProcessCommandLine has "delete shadows"
| project Timestamp, AccountName, ProcessCommandLine
groundTruthSummary: vssadmin delete shadows /all /quiet by SYSTEM on WS-FIN-014. Ransomware-imminent.
playbookReason: Shadow copy deletion = ransomware in seconds. Activate the playbook now.
hunterHints: The /quiet flag suppresses prompts - silent destruction. Look for it explicitly.

ruleDescription: Detects execution of known AD reconnaissance tools (SharpHound, ADExplorer, BloodHound).
starterQuery:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where FileName has_any ("SharpHound", "BloodHound")
| or ProcessCommandLine has_any ("Invoke-BloodHound", "-CollectionMethod")
investigationGoals: What's the user account doing else?
groundTruthSummary: SharpHound.exe -c All --zipfilename loot.zip on WS-FIN-014. AD recon for lateral movement planning.
playbookReason: AD recon means lateral movement is next. The output is gold for IR - preserve before cleanup.
hunterHints: -c All is the SharpHound 'collect everything' flag. --zipfilename names the output.

ruleDescription: EICAR antivirus test string detected. Universal test file - not real malware.
starterQuery:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where SHA256 == "275a021bbfb6489e54d471899f7db9d1663fc695ec2fe2a2c4538aabf651fd0f"
groundTruthSummary: Scheduled control test by SOC analyst on their own machine.
playbookReason: EICAR is the universal AV test - not malicious. Verify scheduled and close.
hunterHints: The SHA256 for EICAR is universal - 275a021bbfb6489e54d471899f7db9d1663fc695ec2fe2a2c4538aabf651fd0f. Memorise it - you'll see it monthly.

ruleDescription: New inbox rule with characteristics typical of BEC: forward-to-external, finance keywords, hidden/single-character name.
starterQuery:
OfficeActivity
| where Operation == "New-InboxRule"
| project TimeGenerated, UserId, ClientIP, Parameters
groundTruthSummary: Rule named '.' forwards invoice/wire/payment emails to attacker@protonmail.com. Created from Bucharest IP.
playbookReason: Hallmarks of BEC: single-char name (hides in UI), finance keywords, external forward, foreign IP.
hunterHints: Parameters field contains the rule details. Look for ForwardTo, SubjectContainsWords.
Cross-reference ClientIP with TI table.

ruleDescription: Abnormal rate of FileDeleted operations from a single user.
starterQuery:
OfficeActivity
| where Operation == "FileDeleted"
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
| summarize Count=count() by UserId, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
groundTruthSummary: 1,247 files deleted in 4 minutes from compromised account. Pre-ransomware/destruction.
playbookReason: Mass deletion at this rate is automated. Connected to a compromised account - destructive activity.
hunterHints: Use bin() to bucket by time and count() to spot the spike.
OneDrive retention typically gives you 93 days to recover.

ruleDescription: User consented to a third-party application requesting high-privilege scopes from an unverified publisher.
starterQuery:
OfficeActivity
| where Operation == "Consent to application"
| project TimeGenerated, UserId, ClientIP, Parameters
groundTruthSummary: User consented to 'DocSign Helper' with Mail.ReadWrite + Files.Read.All + offline_access. Unverified publisher.
playbookReason: Illicit consent grants bypass MFA - the token persists until revoked. Audit access before killing the token.
hunterHints: Scopes containing Mail.ReadWrite or Files.Read.All are red flags from unverified apps.
offline_access means refresh tokens - long-term persistence.

ruleName: Anonymous SharePoint link to sensitive document
ruleDescription: Anonymous (anyone-with-link) sharing created on a file matching sensitivity classifiers.
starterQuery:
OfficeActivity
| where Operation == "AnonymousLinkCreated"
| project TimeGenerated, UserId, SourceFileName, ClientIP, Parameters
groundTruthSummary: MA_Project_Confidential.pdf shared with anyone-with-link by compromised account. Exfil via 'innocent' link.
playbookReason: Anonymous link from a compromised account targeting M&A material = exfil. Audit who fetched the file before revocation.
hunterHints: SourceFileName field contains the filename. Anything with 'Confidential', 'MA_', 'Q1_Earnings', or similar deserves a second look.

ruleDescription: Compliance / eDiscovery export performed by an account that gained admin privileges within the last 7 days.
starterQuery:
OfficeActivity
| where Operation == "SearchExportDownloaded"
| project TimeGenerated, UserId, ClientIP, Parameters
groundTruthSummary: 2-day-old admin account exported all executive mailboxes (18,420 items). No ticket.
playbookReason: Brand-new admin doing privileged exports is a major red flag. Legal-grade exfil potential.
hunterHints: Cross-check the user against AzureActivity for when they were granted admin role - new = suspicious.

ruleName: Public access enabled on storage account
ruleDescription: Storage account configuration changed to allow anonymous public access. Possible data exposure.
starterQuery:
AzureActivity
| where OperationNameValue contains "STORAGEACCOUNTS/WRITE"
| where ResourceId has "stgprodbackups01"
| project TimeGenerated, Caller, CallerIpAddress, Properties
groundTruthSummary: stgprodbackups01 (DB backups) made publicly accessible by sp-backup-rotator from C2 IP.
redHerrings: Open a Jira ticket for next sprint
playbookReason: Public exposure of backups = breach precondition. Containment first, identity second.
hunterHints: Properties field has the actual setting - look for publicAccess or allowBlobPublicAccess.

ruleName: Just-in-Time VM access from external IP
ruleDescription: JIT access requested from an IP not on the standard admin allowlist.
starterQuery:
AzureActivity
| where OperationNameValue contains "JITNETWORKACCESSPOLICIES"
| project TimeGenerated, Caller, CallerIpAddress, ResourceId, Properties
groundTruthSummary: JIT for jumpbox RDP requested from 185.220.101.42 - the C2 IP seen across multiple alerts.
playbookReason: IP is correlated across multiple alerts. Attacker is pivoting endpoint -> identity -> cloud.
hunterHints: Cross-reference the CallerIpAddress against ThreatIntelligenceIndicator and against SigninLogs for other suspicious activity.

ruleName: Key Vault secret accessed from unusual IP
ruleDescription: Key Vault secret read from an IP not seen accessing this vault before.
starterQuery:
AzureActivity
| where OperationNameValue contains "KEYVAULT"
| where ResourceId has "kv-prod-secrets"
groundTruthSummary: db-conn-string-prod read by compromised SP from C2 IP. All accessed secrets are burned.
playbookReason: If attacker reached Key Vault, every secret accessed must be rotated. Downstream apps will need redeployment.
hunterHints: Properties field has the secretName. That's what you rotate.

ruleName: NSG rule allows wide inbound access
ruleDescription: Network Security Group rule allows inbound traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 on a management port.
starterQuery:
AzureActivity
| where OperationNameValue contains "NETWORKSECURITYGROUPS/SECURITYRULES/WRITE"
| project TimeGenerated, Caller, Properties
groundTruthSummary: Developer enabled SSH (22) from 0.0.0.0/0 to prod web subnet. Misconfiguration, not malicious - but unacceptable risk.
playbookReason: Treat as misconfiguration with breach potential. Remediate, then handle the human side.
hunterHints: Properties shows sourceAddressPrefix - 0.0.0.0/0 means anywhere. Combined with port 22 or 3389, it's a panic.

ruleDescription: Process matching crypto-mining signatures detected on cloud VM.
starterQuery:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where DeviceName == "vm-build-runner-04"
| where FileName has "xmrig" or ProcessCommandLine has "pool.minexmr"
investigationGoals: How did it get there (initiating process)?
groundTruthSummary: xmrig running as jenkins, dropped via curl from 91.243.59.110. Foothold is exposed Jenkins webhook.
playbookReason: Mining is the symptom. The entry point matters more. Snapshot before remediation.
hunterHints: The InitiatingProcessCommandLine shows the curl|bash - that's your entry point.

ruleDescription: Email from a lookalike domain delivered with attachment matching malicious patterns.
starterQuery:
OfficeActivity
| where Operation == "MailItemsAccessed"
| where Parameters has "contos0.com"
| project TimeGenerated, UserId, Parameters
groundTruthSummary: billing@contos0.com (typosquat) sent Invoice_April2026.docm to j.harrington. Opened 7 min later. Patient zero email.
playbookReason: This is the entry. Connect to INC-2026-0506 (Office spawned PS) and the rest of the chain.
hunterHints: Cross-reference the sender domain against ThreatIntelligenceIndicator.
Look for other users who got similar emails - search Parameters for the attachment name.

ruleDescription: Outbound connection allowed to an IP appearing on threat intelligence feeds.
starterQuery:
AzureFirewallNetworkRule
| where DstIP == "185.220.101.42"
| project TimeGenerated, SrcIP, DstIP, DstPort, BytesOut
groundTruthSummary: 12.4 MB exfiltrated to Cobalt Strike C2 (TI-confirmed). Source = WS-FIN-014.
playbookReason: Active exfil. Block first, capture if possible, isolate host. Connect to the rest of the WS-FIN-014 chain.
hunterHints: Use sum(BytesOut) to spot exfil volume.
DeviceNetworkEvents will show the source host and process name for the same IP.

ruleName: High-volume DNS queries to single domain
ruleDescription: Unusually high volume of DNS queries to subdomains of a single parent. Possible DNS tunneling.
starterQuery:
DeviceNetworkEvents
| where DeviceName == "WS-FIN-014"
| where RemotePort == 53
| where RemoteUrl contains "uefihelper"
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, RemoteUrl
groundTruthSummary: 820 long-subdomain DNS queries to *.queries.uefihelper.xyz by rundll32. DNS tunneling.
playbookReason: Volume + length + single parent = DNS tunneling. Sinkhole at resolver, isolate host.
hunterHints: strlen() on subdomain shows the data-carrying length. Tunnels use long random-looking labels.

ruleDescription: Consistent periodic connections from a single host to a single external IP. Low jitter suggests automation.
starterQuery:
DeviceNetworkEvents
| where DeviceName == "WS-FIN-014"
| where RemoteIP == "185.220.101.42"
| project Timestamp, BytesSent, BytesReceived
groundTruthSummary: 5-minute interval beacons from WS-FIN-014 to C2 IP starting 08:00. Earliest indicator of compromise.
playbookReason: Beaconing is the C2 heartbeat. Catching it at 08:00 would have prevented everything that followed.
hunterHints: bin(Timestamp, 5m) and count() will reveal the pattern. Consistent count per bucket = automation.

ruleDescription: Internal host initiated SMB (port 445) connection to a public IP. Workstations rarely have a legitimate reason.
starterQuery:
DeviceNetworkEvents
| where RemotePort == 445
| where not(RemoteIP startswith "10.") and not(RemoteIP startswith "172.") and not(RemoteIP startswith "192.168.")
groundTruthSummary: WS-DEV-031 SMB to 45.83.193.150. Likely forced authentication for NTLM hash capture.
correctActions: Investigate source for credential theft vector
playbookReason: Outbound SMB leaks NTLM hashes via forced auth even if session fails. Credential theft vector.
hunterHints: RFC1918 ranges are private. Anything else on port 445 outbound is suspicious.

ruleName: Suspicious process execution: Word spawned encoded PowerShell
ruleDescription: Defender for Endpoint detected a PowerShell process spawned by WINWORD.EXE running with an encoded (-EncodedCommand) payload and an outbound connection to a non-whitelisted IP. Classic macro-based execution from a phishing attachment.
starterQuery:
DeviceAlertEvents
| where DeviceName == "WIN10-WS-047"
| where Timestamp > ago(2h)
| project Timestamp, AlertTitle, FileName, InitiatingProcessFileName, ProcessCommandLine, RemoteIP
investigationGoals: What was the encoded command content?
Is this the only affected host?
groundTruthSummary: WINWORD.EXE on WIN10-WS-047 opened Invoice-Q2.docm and spawned powershell.exe -enc <base64>. Decoded command contacts 185.220.101.47. 2.1MB exfiltrated. Single host so far but lateral movement underway.
correctActions: Decode the -enc payload and IOC-share with email security
playbookReason: Word spawning encoded PowerShell with an outbound to a Tor exit is unambiguous. Isolate first, then preserve evidence. Encoded command and process tree are the deliverables for IR.
hunterHints: Start at DeviceAlertEvents for WIN10-WS-047, then pivot to DeviceProcessEvents to walk the parent chain.
ProcessCommandLine contains '-enc' or '-EncodedCommand' — base64 the rest and decode.
DeviceNetworkEvents will show the RemoteIP the powershell.exe process actually called.

ruleName: Multiple mailboxes accessed from anonymizing service
ruleDescription: Multiple finance team mailboxes were accessed in sequence from a single Tor exit node IP. No interactive MFA challenge was recorded for any of the sessions — suggests OAuth token replay or refresh-token theft.
starterQuery:
EmailEvents
| where SrcIP == "185.220.101.47"
| where TimeGenerated > ago(6h)
| project TimeGenerated, Operation, UserId, SrcIP, MailboxId
investigationGoals: How many mailboxes were accessed and by which accounts?
Was this a successful OAuth token theft or credential reuse?
Were any emails forwarded or exfiltrated?
Is MFA enforced on these accounts?
groundTruthSummary: 4 finance mailboxes (ap@, ar@, payroll@, controller@) accessed sequentially from Tor exit 185.220.101.47 09:18–09:22 UTC. No SigninLogs MFA event — replayed refresh token. Two auto-forward rules created. Compromise confirmed.
correctActions: Revoke all sessions for the 4 accounts
Delete the auto-forward rules and audit sent items
redHerrings: Notify users to watch for phishing
playbookReason: Tor + sequential mailbox access + no MFA event = token replay. Password reset alone won't kill the refresh token — revoke sessions. Auto-forward rules are the exfil vector.
hunterHints: EmailEvents filtered by SrcIP shows the full set of mailboxes touched.
Look for Operation == 'New-InboxRule' from the same IP — the persistence move.
Cross-reference 185.220.101.47 against ThreatIntelligenceIndicator — it'll be on Tor exit lists.

ruleName: IDPS: Repeated port scan from internal host
ruleDescription: Azure Firewall IDPS triggered on a high-rate horizontal port scan from an internal host. 847 connection attempts across 22 destination ports inside 4 minutes — pattern matches Nmap default SYN scan timing.
starterQuery:
AzureFirewallNetworkRule
| where SrcIP == "10.0.2.88"
| where TimeGenerated between (datetime(2026-05-13T14:25:00Z) .. datetime(2026-05-13T14:35:00Z))
| summarize Attempts=count(), DistinctPorts=dcount(DstPort), Allowed=countif(Action == "Allow") by SrcIP
investigationGoals: Is 10.0.2.88 the same host as the Defender alert 30 minutes earlier?
What ports were targeted and what services do they correspond to?
Were any connections successful (allowed by firewall)?
Does the scan pattern match a known tool (Nmap, Masscan)?
groundTruthSummary: 10.0.2.88 resolves to WIN10-WS-047 — same host as INC-2026-0531. Scanned 22 ports (22, 80, 135, 139, 389, 443, 445, 1433, 3389, 5985, …) — classic Nmap top-ports list. 6 succeeded on the file server, including SMB (445) and WinRM (5985). Attacker pivoting from initial foothold.
correctActions: Confirm WIN10-WS-047 is already isolated from INC-2026-0531
Investigate the 6 successful connections — focus on the file server
Audit WinRM/SMB sessions on receiving hosts
redHerrings: Wait for IDPS rule to expire
playbookReason: Internal port scan from a host already under suspicion is lateral movement reconnaissance. Following the 6 successful connections is more important than blocking the scanner — the host is already isolated.
hunterHints: bin(TimeGenerated, 30s) and count() will show the scan rate — Nmap defaults to roughly 5/sec.
Group by DstPort then filter Action == 'Allow' to find the successful pivots.
DeviceProcessEvents on the source host shows the scanning binary — likely nmap.exe or a PowerShell port-test loop.

ruleName: Ransomware precursor: Volume shadow copies deleted
ruleDescription: vssadmin.exe executed with 'delete shadows /all /quiet' parameters on two hosts within 30 seconds. Preceded by encoded PowerShell and lateral movement activity. Highest-fidelity pre-ransomware indicator.
starterQuery:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where DeviceName in ("WIN10-WS-047", "FILE-SRV-001")
| where ProcessCommandLine has "vssadmin" and ProcessCommandLine has "delete shadows"
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName, ProcessCommandLine, InitiatingProcessFileName
investigationGoals: Confirm the full attack chain from the earlier PowerShell alert
Has the attacker reached the file server?
Is encryption already underway (look for mass file rename events)?
What is the immediate containment action?
groundTruthSummary: vssadmin delete shadows /all /quiet ran on WIN10-WS-047 at 15:44:18 and FILE-SRV-001 at 15:44:42 — both invoked by SYSTEM via remote PowerShell session from WIN10-WS-047. Mass file rename events follow on the file server within 90 seconds. Active ransomware encryption.
correctActions: Confirm network isolation for WIN10-WS-047 and FILE-SRV-001
Activate ransomware playbook — invoke IR retainer + Legal
Escalate Critical — executive notification per IR plan
playbookReason: Shadow copy deletion is the pre-encryption ritual. By the time you see this alert, encryption has either started or is seconds away. Isolation and backup verification are now in parallel — there is no investigation phase here, only containment.
hunterHints: /quiet flag suppresses the prompt — search for it specifically.
InitiatingProcessFileName will likely be powershell.exe or wmiexec.py — that's your lateral movement vector.
Look for the same command on multiple hosts within the same minute — that's the scope of the attacker's foothold.

ruleName: Privileged role assigned outside change window
ruleDescription: Service account svc-backup@contoso.com was assigned the Global Administrator role at 14:53 UTC. No matching change ticket in ServiceNow. The assigning account was last seen signing in from a Tor exit node 6 hours earlier.
starterQuery:
AzureActivity
| where OperationNameValue contains "ROLEASSIGNMENTS/WRITE"
| where TimeGenerated > ago(2h)
| project TimeGenerated, Caller, CallerIpAddress, Properties
investigationGoals: Who assigned the role and from what IP/session?
Is the assigning account itself compromised (trace back to INC-2026-0532)?
What actions has svc-backup taken since the role assignment?
Has the role been removed? If not, what is the immediate action?
groundTruthSummary: finance-controller@contoso.com (compromised in INC-2026-0532) granted Global Administrator to svc-backup@contoso.com at 14:53. svc-backup then created an app registration with Mail.ReadWrite and disabled conditional access on itself. Active privilege escalation pivoting from BEC.
correctActions: Remove Global Administrator role from svc-backup immediately
Audit and revoke any app registrations / API permissions created in the window
playbookReason: Role assignment from a compromised identity to a service account is a textbook persistence move. Service accounts are rarely watched, GA gives full tenant control. Containment is identity-first: kill the sessions, then unwind any objects created.
hunterHints: Pivot the Caller field against SigninLogs from the last 24h — was the assigning account on Tor recently?
Properties contains the role definition ID — '62e90394-69f5-4237-9190-012177145e10' is Global Administrator.
AzureActivity for Caller == 'svc-backup@contoso.com' shows what they did with the new privilege.
